bibliographic_data.title: Presenting and Evaluating the Impact of Experiential Learning in Computing Accessibility Education
bibliographic_data.abstract: Studies indicate that much of the software created today is not accessible to all users, indicating that developers don’t see the need to devote sufficient resources to creating accessible software. Compounding this problem, there is a lack of robust, easily adoptable educational accessibility material available to instructors for inclusion in their curricula. To address these issues, we have created five Accessibility Learning Labs (ALL) using an experiential learning structure. The labs are designed to educate and create awareness of accessibility needs in computing. The labs enable easy classroom integration by providing instructors with complete educational materials including lecture slides, activities, and quizzes. The labs are hosted on our servers and require only a browser to be utilized. To demonstrate the benefit of our material and the potential benefits of our experiential lab format with empathy-creating material, we conducted a study involving 276 students in ten sections of an introductory computing course. Our findings include: (I) The demonstrated potential of the proposed experiential learning format and labs are effective in motivating and educating students about the importance of accessibility; (II) The labs are effective in informing students about foundational accessibility topics; (III) Empathy-creating material is demonstrated to be a beneficial component in computing accessibility education, supporting students in placing a higher value on the importance of creating accessible software.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents and evaluates the impact of experiential learning in computing accessibility education through the creation of five Accessibility Learning Labs (ALL) that are designed to educate students about foundational accessibility topics and create awareness of the need for accessible software.
core_content.key_methodology: Five Accessibility Learning Labs were created using an experiential learning structure. These labs include relevant background information, example applications with accessibility problems, processes to emulate these issues, technical details on how to repair them, and empathy-creating material from users with disabilities.
outcomes: The proposed experiential learning format and labs are effective in motivating students about the importance of accessibility
The proposed material is effective in informing students about foundational accessibility topics
Empathy-creating material is demonstrated to be a beneficial component in computing accessibility education
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper addresses the issue that much software today is not accessible, indicating a lack of developer awareness and robust educational materials for instructors. To tackle this problem, five Accessibility Learning Labs (ALL) were created using an experiential learning structure. These labs are designed to educate students about foundational accessibility topics and create awareness of the need for accessible software. The labs provide complete educational materials including lecture slides, activities, and quizzes, hosted on servers requiring only a browser. A study involving 276 students in ten sections of an introductory computing course demonstrated that (I) the proposed experiential learning format is effective in motivating students about accessibility; (II) the material effectively informs students about foundational topics; and (III) empathy-creating material supports placing higher value on creating accessible software.
